title: SEGMENT 8 — Where the stem cells come from: marrow, blood, or cord

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Where the stem cells come from: marrow, blood, or cord
The label "bone marrow transplant" is, in 2026, often a misnomer.
Most transplants today are not bone marrow transplants in the literal sense.
The cells are no longer typically harvested from the bone marrow itself.
Over the last thirty years, the field has shifted to using stem cells collected from one of three sources, each with its own properties, advantages, and tradeoffs.
Let me walk through them.
Source one: bone marrow harvest.
This is the original method, dating back to the Thomas era.
The donor goes to the operating room under general anesthesia.

A surgeon uses a large-bore needle to repeatedly aspirate marrow from the posterior iliac crests — the back of the hip bones — often making fifty to a hundred separate punctures to collect approximately one liter of marrow.
The marrow is rich in HSCs, but also rich in mature blood cells and stromal cells; it's a complex mixture.
The collection is then processed, filtered, sometimes washed or T-cell-depleted, and infused into the recipient.
For the donor, the procedure means a hospital stay, general anesthesia, post-procedure pain and bruising at the harvest sites, and a recovery of a few weeks.
It is not a trivial procedure for the donor, but it is well-tolerated by healthy volunteers and serious complications are rare.
Bone marrow harvest is still used today, particularly for pediatric recipients and in some haploidentical regimens, but it is no longer the dominant method.
Source two: peripheral blood stem cells, or PBSC.
This is now the most common source.
Here's the trick.

Under normal conditions, HSCs are anchored in the bone marrow niches by sticky interactions — chemokine signaling between CXCL12 from the niche and CXCR4 on the stem cells, plus adhesion molecules like VLA-4.

But if you give the donor a few days of G-CSF — granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, a hormone that drives marrow proliferation — the marrow gets overstuffed, the CXCL12-CXCR4 anchoring breaks down, and HSCs are released into the bloodstream.
They are "mobilized" out of their niches into the peripheral circulation.
The donor then sits in a chair for four to six hours connected to an apheresis machine — a centrifuge that separates the white cell fraction from the blood and returns the rest to the donor.
The collected white cells contain enough HSCs to make a transplant dose.
No surgery.
No general anesthesia.
The donor experiences a few days of bone aches from the G-CSF, but recovers within a week.
This is now the most common collection method, used in roughly 75 percent of adult allogeneic transplants and the great majority of autologous transplants.
The grafts contain more HSCs and many more T cells than marrow grafts, which means faster engraftment but somewhat higher rates of chronic GVHD.

For autologous transplant — including the gene therapy applications — PBSC is essentially universal, because you can mobilize the patient with G-CSF, sometimes augmented with a CXCR4 antagonist called plerixafor that further disrupts the niche anchoring, and collect huge numbers of stem cells in one or two apheresis sessions.
Source three: umbilical cord blood.
The blood remaining in the placenta and umbilical cord after a baby is born is rich in HSCs — these are the stem cells that just finished building the baby's blood and immune system in utero.
After the baby's cord is clamped, the residual blood can be collected from the cord and placenta, HLA-typed, and frozen for later use.

Cord blood banks have stored hundreds of thousands of units worldwide, both public — donated and available to any matched patient — and private, family-banked at the parents' expense for potential future use by the child or a sibling.

Cord blood as a transplant source was pioneered by Eliane Gluckman in Paris in 1988, when the first successful cord blood transplant treated a child with Fanconi anemia using cord blood from his newborn sister.
Cord blood has two big advantages and one big disadvantage.

Advantages: the HSCs are immunologically naive — they haven't yet learned to discriminate self from non-self — which means cord blood transplants tolerate more HLA mismatch than adult donor transplants.
A 4/6 cord match can engraft where a 9/10 adult unrelated mismatch would not.
This is enormously important for ethnic minority patients underserved by adult registries.
And cord blood is available off the shelf — once banked, you can ship a unit overnight, rather than waiting weeks for an adult donor to be worked up.
Disadvantage: the cell dose is small.
A single cord blood unit contains roughly one-tenth the cell dose of an adult marrow or PBSC harvest.
This means slower engraftment — sometimes 30 to 40 days versus 14 to 21 — and higher rates of graft failure, especially in adult recipients.
Strategies to address this include double cord transplant (using two units together), cord blood expansion ex vivo, and intra-bone delivery.
So the three sources, in summary: bone marrow — the original, requires surgical harvest, still used selectively.
Peripheral blood stem cells — mobilized with G-CSF, collected by apheresis, dominant today.
Cord blood — banked at birth, smaller dose but more tolerant of mismatch, particularly valuable for minority patients.

Each source produces a graft with somewhat different cellular composition — different ratios of CD34+ stem cells, T cells, NK cells, monocytes — and those differences subtly affect engraftment kinetics, GVHD risk, and graft-versus-tumor effect.
It is one of the small art forms of transplant oncology to choose the right source for the right patient with the right disease.
For the gene therapy applications — Casgevy, Zynteglo, and the rest — the source is essentially always autologous mobilized peripheral blood.

The patient gets G-CSF and plerixafor, undergoes apheresis to collect billions of CD34+ cells, those cells are shipped to the manufacturing facility, edited with CRISPR or transduced with a lentiviral vector, returned to the hospital, and re-infused after conditioning.
The collection step is exactly the same as for autologous transplant in lymphoma.
The difference is what happens in between.
